title: Chapter 28: Contested
content: The email arrives at 11:47 PM on a Tuesday, because of course it does. Debra Wu has always loved the late-night delivery—the grenade rolled silently into someone's inbox while they're half-asleep and defenseless. Chloe used to admire that about her. Used to think it was strategic.
Now she knows it's just mean.
Dave's laptop glows on the kitchen counter. He's in the other room, mixing something for a client, headphones on, completely unaware. Chloe reads the subject line three times before it registers.
**FORMAL CEASE AND DESIST — @CAPYBARADAILY IP INFRINGEMENT PENDING ARBITRATION**

Her stomach drops. Or whatever the capybara equivalent of a stomach drop is—an entire torso clenching like a fist, fur standing on end, the sudden urge to submerge herself in the nearest body of water and stay there.

She can't scroll. She doesn't have fingers. But the email preview shows enough: *...intellectual property developed during the course of employment... brand identity substantially derived from... failure to transfer account constitutes...*
She screams. It comes out as a bark—sharp, surprisingly loud. Dave's headphones must not be that loud, because he's in the kitchen in four seconds, pulling them off his neck with one hand.
"What? What happened?"
Chloe gestures at the screen with her whole head. Dave leans in, squints, and starts reading.

It takes him a while. The cease-and-desist is twelve pages. Twelve pages of legalese, formatted in that particular shade of corporate aggressive that says *we paid $800 an hour for this specifically to make you feel small*. Attachments bulge below the email: exhibits, evidence, screenshots.
Dave sits down heavily.
"She's claiming the TikTok account is her intellectual property," he says, voice flat. "Because you—because Chloe developed 'brand identity concepts' while employed at Seraph Wellness."

Chloe makes a sound that is definitely not a word. It's somewhere between a groan and a hiss, the kind of noise that escapes when you realize someone's been building a case against you for months and you didn't even know you were in a courtroom.
"Let me see the exhibits."

She can't click. Dave clicks for her, scrolling through the attachments with the careful patience of someone defusing a bomb. Exhibit A: screenshots of Chloe's old PR proposals from Seraph Wellness. Her campaign decks. The ones she'd poured herself into for three years, trying to make wellness feel less like a scam and more like genuine care. The ones Debra had approved and then gutted and then taken credit for.

There, in black and white (well, in Seraph Wellness brand blue and white), is Chloe's own work. Her fonts. Her mood boards. Her language—**"authentic connection," "community-first wellness," "meeting people where they are."**
The same language Chloe uses on @capybaradaily. Because it's her language. She wrote it. She believed it before Debra paid her to believe it and kept believing it after Debra made it meaningless.
But in the context of a legal filing, with the timestamps and the letterhead and the *Exhibit A* heading, it looks like theft.
"She's framing it like I stole proprietary concepts," Chloe says. Her voice is quiet. "Like my own thoughts belong to her because she paid me to think them once."
Dave nods slowly. He's still reading. His jaw tightens.

Dave's lawyer friend—the one he kept insisting was "just a guy I know, not like, a LAWYER lawyer"—turns out to be an actual lawyer. Named Karen, which feels cosmically unfair. She calls on speakerphone the next morning, and her voice has the unhurried confidence of someone who bills in six-minute increments.
"Okay, I've read the filing. Here's the good news and the bad news."
"Bad news first," Dave says. Chloe is curled next to the laptop, ears flat, listening.

"The bad news is this is bullshit, but it's expensive bullshit. She's claiming you developed the account's brand architecture using trade skills and conceptual frameworks from your employment. It's a stretch—honestly, it's a contortion—but IP law is messy enough that she can file this and force you to respond. She can drag this out for a year. Depositions, discovery, expert witnesses. You'd spend twenty thousand dollars proving you don't owe her anything. Maybe more."
Chloe closes her eyes. Twenty thousand dollars. Her entire TikTok revenue is thirty-four hundred a month. That's six months of income burned on proving a negative.
"The good news?" Dave asks.

"The good news is she almost certainly won't win on the merits. The TikTok account didn't exist when Chloe worked for her. There's no employment contract clause covering social media accounts created after termination. And the 'brand development' theory is paper-thin—you can't copyright a vibe. But—"
"But she doesn't need to win," Chloe says, opening her eyes. "She needs to cost."
A pause on the line. Then, "Yeah. That's exactly right."

Chloe knows this play. She's done this play. Not at this scale, not with lawyers, but the same principle: make it expensive enough to fight and cheap enough to settle. Bleed them until capitulation looks like relief. She once watched Debra do it to a smaller wellness brand that used the word "serene" in their packaging. Debra didn't own "serene." She just owned more lawyers.
"Karen," Dave says carefully. "What happens to Chloe's TikTok revenue while this is pending?"
Another pause. Longer this time.

"The filing includes a request for injunctive relief on the account. Pending resolution, the platform may freeze monetization. It's—yeah. It's a standard play. Cut off the revenue stream, increase the pressure to settle."
Chloe is already checking her phone. The Creator Fund dashboard loads, spins, and delivers the verdict: **Payout suspended pending IP dispute resolution. Estimated timeline: 60-90 days.**
Thirty-four hundred dollars a month. Gone. Frozen. Sitting in escrow somewhere, paying for nobody's rent.
She puts her head down on the counter. The cool granite feels good against her fur. She stays there.

Maya shows up that evening with paint in her hair and fury in her eyes. She doesn't knock—she hasn't knocked since week two—and she walks in carrying a canvas she's been working on, wet paint still glistening.

"Dave told me. I was already painting when he called, and now I'm painting angrier, which honestly, better technique. Look at this brushwork." She holds up the canvas. It's abstract—sharp red lines cutting through soft green, the contrast violent and beautiful. "It's called 'Corporate Gaslighting' and it's going in the living room."
Chloe appreciates this. She doesn't have the energy to say so, but Maya reads it in the way Chloe's nose twitches.

"You know what gets me?" Maya sets the painting down, leans against the counter. "She's claiming you like—like you're her creation. Like you're a product she developed. You were a person before you were a capybara, Chloe. The TikTok didn't even exist when you worked for her. Your *body* didn't even—" She stops, recalibrates. "Your brain is yours. Your thoughts are yours. The way you see the world is yours. She doesn't get to own that because she cut your paychecks for three years."

"I used her language," Chloe says quietly. "The 'community first' stuff. The 'authentic connection' framing. It's in her exhibits and it's—she's not wrong that I developed those concepts on her dime."

"You developed those concepts in your head," Maya says. "She paid you for your time. She didn't buy your brain. That's not how employment works." A beat. "That's not how anything works, except like, maybe the military?"

"Noncompetes can cover intellectual scope," Chloe says, and she hates how much she knows about this. Hates that three years of proximity to Debra's legal strategy taught her exactly how these traps are built. "If she can argue the account's success is derivative of skills I developed in her employ—"

"Then every chef who opens a restaurant owes their old boss," Maya interrupts. "Every writer who gets published owes their MFA program. Chloe. She's not making a legal argument. She's making a *hostage* argument. She's holding your money and hoping you'll fold."

Chloe knows this. She knows all of this. She's been on the other side of this exact spreadsheet, watching Debra calculate exactly how much pressure to apply before someone breaks. The difference is that when Chloe was watching, she was sitting in a climate-controlled office with a matching 401k. Now she's a capybara with frozen bank accounts and no legal standing in any jurisdiction that matters.
"She doesn't need to win," Chloe says again, and this time it sounds less like legal analysis and more like the plain, simple truth it is. "She needs to cost."
Maya sits down on the floor next to her. Doesn't say anything for a long time. Just sits. The canvas drips red onto Dave's kitchen tile.
That night, Chloe can't sleep. She lies on her cushion—the nice one Maya bought her, the one that matches nothing in the apartment but is the exact right firmness—and stares at the ceiling.

She runs the numbers. Rent: $1,800. Groceries: $400, which is insane for one person but she eats a lot of produce now, capybara metabolism is no joke. Utilities: $220. Dave doesn't charge rent anymore—hasn't since the TikTok started paying—but he also can't float her forever. His sound engineering work is freelance, and freelance is feast-or-famine, and he's been spending a lot of time on capybara logistics instead of client calls.
Without the TikTok money: she has $2,100 in savings. That's five weeks. Maybe six, if she stops buying the good lettuce.

She could take a settlement. Debra would offer one—probably already has the terms drafted. Sign over the account, walk away, maybe get a few thousand dollars for "goodwill." It would be the smart play. The efficient play. The play Chloe-the-PR-professional would have advised any client to take.

But Chloe-the-capybara has forty-seven thousand followers who send her messages like "your page is the only good thing on my feed" and "I was having a panic attack and your video about just existing in a patch of sun actually helped" and "thank you for making it okay to not be okay."
She can't give them to Debra. She can't hand Debra a community built on the one thing Debra has never understood: that authenticity isn't a strategy. It's just what's left when you stop performing.
The debt was abstract before. An idea. A legal filing. Paper and email and exhibits she could pretend weren't real.
Now it's a weapon. Now it has a handle, and Debra's holding it, and the blade is Chloe's own words forged into something sharp.
She puts her head down on the cushion and does the only thing that helps.
No thoughts. Just fluff.
It doesn't work.
